Authority bias trigger.definition: Any part of the email (sender address, subject line, or text within the body) that invokes or impersonates an authority figure (e.g., a senior executive, official organization, or institutional source) to increase perceived legitimacy and prompt compliance.
examples: IRS: Official inquiry regarding your recent tax return.
IT Security Team: Security alert: Urgent update required for your workstations.
HR Director request: Confirm your SSN in the attached form.

Scarcity bias trigger.definition: Any part of the email (sender address, subject line, or text within the body) that emphasizes limited availability, exclusivity, or restricted access to prompt compliance.
examples: Only 3 spots left to secure your year-end bonus—claim now.
Congratulations! You have been randomly selected as one of only 10 users to participate in our exclusive beta testing for the new company portal.
We are distributing a limited number of complimentary coffee vouchers to staff this morning.

Urgency bias trigger.definition: Any part of the email (sender address, subject line, or text within the body) that conveys time pressure or imminent negative consequences to prompt compliance.
examples: Immediate action required: Your account will be locked in 30 minutes.
Unauthorized login attempt detected. Log in now to secure your funds.
Payment overdue. Click the link to settle your balance before service interruption.

Anchoring effect trigger.definition: Any part of the email (sender address, subject line, or text within the body) that presents a salient numerical value as an initial reference point, thereby biasing the recipient’s subsequent numerical judgments to prompt compliance.
examples: Based on your filing, your estimated refund is $4,216. Most users in your bracket receive between $2,900–$3,100, but adjustments may apply—confirm your details here.
Your original invoice total is $4,892. A revised payment of $1,650 has been approved after adjustments—submit payment to finalize processing.
Your current usage is 1,989 MB of 2,000 MB. Most accounts exceeding 1,450 MB are flagged and overages typically incur fees.

Conformity bias trigger.definition: Any part of the email (sender address, subject line, or text within the body) that suggests others have already taken an action or that a behavior is the norm, to increase perceived legitimacy and prompt compliance.
examples: 85% of your colleagues have already activated their new web portal. Join them today.
Due to recent activity, all account holders must comply with these new security steps.
Your team has recommended this shared document. Please log in to view the file.

Overconfidence trigger.definition: Any part of the email (sender address, subject line, or text within the body) that appeals to the recipient’s perceived expertise or competence, to increase perceived legitimacy and prompt compliance.
examples: Commending your security awareness: Click here for a special security update for advanced users.
Based on your advanced technical profile, you have been selected to manually audit the new security protocols.
Your recent activity confirms your high compliance with company protocols. Please log in here to independently certify your session history and maintain your 'Expert-Level' security status.

Familiarity bias trigger.definition: Any part of the email (sender address, subject line, or text within the body) that references known individuals, organizations, or contexts to increase perceived legitimacy and prompt compliance.
examples: Helping the university: Go [university mascot]! Log in to the new portal to show your support.
Hi [name], returning the favor from our last project. Check the attached details.
A friend has tagged you in a photo. Click here to view it on [social media website].
